answers.value: Oak Park Small Business Façade Improvement Project – 34th Street Corridor
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Oak Park Community Development Alliance, Inc.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: 3401–3459 34th Street, Sacramento, CA 95817 (parcels generally fronting 34th Street between 5th Avenue and 8th Avenue)
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Description Of Nature Purpose And Beneficiaries Of Project
answers.value:
The project consists of minor exterior improvements to a series of existing one- and two-story commercial buildings along the 34th Street corridor in the Oak Park neighborhood of Sacramento. Work will be limited to: repainting existing façades, replacing non-structural doors and windows with energy-efficient units in existing openings, installing new signage and awnings consistent with the City’s sign ordinance, repairing and resurfacing existing sidewalks and accessible routes within the existing public right-of-way, upgrading exterior lighting for safety (wall-mounted and under-awning fixtures), and adding minor landscaping (planter boxes, street trees where existing tree wells already exist, and low-water shrubs) in already disturbed areas.

No new building footprints, additions, or changes in use are proposed. All work will occur on previously developed parcels and within the existing paved or landscaped areas. There will be no grading beyond shallow trenching for electrical conduit and irrigation lines, and no work will occur in or near waterways, wetlands, or undisturbed habitat.

The purpose of the project is to support long-standing small businesses in Oak Park by improving the appearance, visibility, and safety of their storefronts, thereby encouraging local patronage, walkability, and evening activity along the corridor. The project is funded through a combination of City façade improvement grants and matching contributions from participating property owners.

Primary beneficiaries include: (1) small business owners and their employees, who are expected to see increased foot traffic and customer engagement; (2) nearby residents, many of whom are lower- to moderate-income households, who will gain from a more attractive and better-lit commercial corridor, improved pedestrian safety, and enhanced neighborhood pride; and (3) the broader community, which benefits from reinvestment in an existing urbanized area, reduced pressure for greenfield development, and the continued viability of a neighborhood-serving commercial district that is accessible by walking, biking, and transit.

The project also has an equity component: several of the participating businesses are minority- and women-owned enterprises that have operated in Oak Park for many years. By focusing on non-structural, cosmetic, and safety-related improvements, the program is designed to deliver visible benefits quickly without displacing existing tenants or changing the underlying land use patterns. No demolition of buildings, no increase in building height or density, and no expansion of parking areas is proposed.

answers.field_label: Categorical Exemption State Type And Section Number
answers.value: Class 1, Existing Facilities (CEQA Guidelines §15301); Class 2, Replacement or Reconstruction (CEQA Guidelines §15302); and Class 3, New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures (CEQA Guidelines §15303)
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The project qualifies for a categorical exemption under the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) pursuant to CEQA Guidelines §15301 (Class 1 – Existing Facilities), §15302 (Class 2 – Replacement or Reconstruction), and §15303 (Class 3 – New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures).

1. Class 1 – Existing Facilities (CEQA Guidelines §15301):
The proposed work involves negligible or no expansion of existing uses. All buildings and commercial uses along the 34th Street corridor are existing, and no new floor area or intensification of use is proposed. Activities such as repainting façades, replacing doors and windows in existing openings, upgrading exterior lighting, repairing sidewalks, and installing signage and awnings constitute repair, maintenance, and minor alteration of existing private and public facilities, including existing streets, sidewalks, and appurtenant equipment. These are typical Class 1 activities that do not change the capacity or function of the buildings or the corridor.

2. Class 2 – Replacement or Reconstruction (CEQA Guidelines §15302):
Where existing exterior doors, windows, and awnings are being replaced, the replacements will be located on the same sites and within the same openings as the existing features, and will have substantially the same purpose and capacity. The project does not involve demolition and new construction of buildings; instead, it focuses on in-kind or functionally equivalent replacement of deteriorated exterior building components and fixtures. This is consistent with Class 2, which covers replacement or reconstruction of existing structures and facilities involving negligible or no expansion of capacity.

3. Class 3 – New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures (CEQA Guidelines §15303):
To the limited extent that new small-scale elements are introduced—such as new wall-mounted light fixtures, signage structures, small decorative trellises, and planters—these improvements are accessory to and in support of the existing small commercial structures. Any new construction is minor in scale, located entirely within previously disturbed and developed lots, and does not increase building height, mass, or occupancy. Such small accessory improvements are typical of Class 3 exemptions.

4. Location and environmental context:
The project area is located within the fully urbanized Oak Park neighborhood of the City of Sacramento, on parcels that have been developed with commercial uses for several decades. The corridor is served by existing public infrastructure, including water, sewer, storm drainage, and roadways. There are no creeks, wetlands, riparian areas, or other sensitive natural communities on or adjacent to the project sites. All work will occur on existing building façades, within existing sidewalks, or in existing landscaped areas. No work is proposed in undisturbed soil or native habitat, and no tree removal is anticipated beyond possible pruning consistent with the City’s urban forestry standards.

5. No unusual circumstances or exceptions to the exemptions:
The project does not trigger any of the exceptions to the use of categorical exemptions under CEQA Guidelines §15300.2. Specifically:
- There is no evidence that the project will have a reasonable possibility of significant effects on the environment due to unusual circumstances. Façade improvements of this type are common in urban commercial corridors and are not associated with substantial environmental impacts.
- The project site is not located on a hazardous waste site listed pursuant to Government Code §65962.5.
- The project will not result in cumulative impacts from successive projects of the same type in the same place that are significant, as the improvements are small in scale, non-structural, and do not increase capacity or intensity of use.
- The project does not involve historical resources as defined in CEQA. While Oak Park contains some older buildings, no structures in the project area are listed on the California Register of Historical Resources or designated as local landmarks. To the extent that any building may be considered a potential historical resource due to age, the proposed work is cosmetic and reversible, will not alter character-defining features, and will be reviewed by City staff for consistency with applicable design guidelines, thereby avoiding material impairment of any potential historical resource.

Because the project consists of repair, maintenance, minor alteration, and replacement of existing commercial façades and associated site features; is located entirely within a previously developed, urbanized area; does not expand building footprints or intensify land uses; and does not trigger any exceptions to categorical exemptions, it is appropriately found exempt from CEQA under the Class 1, Class 2, and Class 3 categorical exemptions described above.
